title: Random neural networks match observed dimensionality of neural population recordings and motivate stronger experimental tests
authors: Zehui Zhao, Michael J Pasek, Ilya M Nemenman
abstract: Randomly connected neural networks have long served as a theoretical tool for studying collective dynamics in neural populations, yet quantitative comparisons to experiments remain limited. Recent technological advances have made it possible to resolve population-wide correlations across neurons, and minimal models such as random neural networks predict their generic structure. Whether the two agree quantitatively remains untested. In this work, we examine whether a minimally structured random neural network can account for the low dimensionality of activity in neural population recordings by building on recent developments in Dynamical Mean-Field Theory and incorporating two additional experimentally relevant features into the model: finite measurement time and variability across behavioral contexts. We show that, when these factors are included, the dimensionality measured from large-scale recordings is consistent with the values predicted by random models. However, current recording durations make it difficult to use dimensionality to discriminate among connectivity structures. We further show that analytically predicted dimensionality varies non-monotonically with external input strength, and that the orientation similarity between neural manifolds recorded under different behavioral contexts can be more sensitive to network structure than dimensionality is. Together, these results provide quantitative guidance for experimental design to infer the connectivity structure underlying population activity.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the low-dimensional activity observed in biological neural populations can be explained by simple, randomly connected networks when accounting for finite measurement times and external inputs. It suggests that researchers should look beyond absolute dimensionality and instead use metrics like the orientation of activity fluctuations to identify true underlying network structures.
main_problem: Determining whether the observed low dimensionality in neural population activity is a result of specialized brain wiring or a generic property of randomly connected networks, and evaluating if current experimental measurements are sufficient to distinguish between them.
main_result: Random neural networks can match observed dimensionality in primate motor cortex, but current recording durations are too short to use dimensionality alone to distinguish connectivity structures; instead, measuring orientation similarity or non-monotonicity of fluctuations provides stronger tests.
method: The authors use Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) and a two-replica approach to semi-analytically calculate dimensionality and covariance statistics, supplemented by numerical simulations of large-scale networks.
model_or_system: A population of N neurons with recurrent interactions described by a Sompolinsky-style random recurrent neural network model, where connectivity is sampled from a Gaussian distribution and neurons are driven by external behavioral contexts.
key_observables: Dimensionality (Participation Ratio), cosine similarity of ordered responses, orientation similarity of fluctuation ellipsoids, and population variances (ordered and chaotic components).
important_parameters: Gain parameter (g), external input strength (I), measurement time (T), autocorrelation time (tau_C), and neuron number (N).
main_assumptions: Uses an adiabatic approximation for external inputs, assumes a specific error function nonlinearity, and assumes the large-network limit (N to infinity) for analytical derivations.
figures_summary: Figures show the dependence of finite-time dimensionality on measurement time, the non-monotonic relationship between input strength and dimensionality, and the stability of orientation similarity and cosine similarity across different regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of dimensionality in neural populations, presents a random network model using DMFT, analyzes the effects of finite measurement time and input strength, compares results to experimental primate data, and proposes new experimental diagnostics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Statistics of activity under a single behavioral context. A: Schematic: blue curves represent the temporal chaos, approximated as an ellipsoid in state space; the yellow cross marks the ordered response at the ellipsoid’s center. B: The variance of temporal chaos ˜C0 decreases with external input strength I, while the variance of the ordered response ¯C increases with I, as indicated by the legend. The ordered-response variance ¯C shows a cusp at the transition from chaotic to ordered dynamics, where ˜C0 = 0, marked by the vertical dashed line. For g = 3, the transition happens around I ≈4.15. Markers with error bars represent simulation results (mean and standard deviation over...

Figure 2: Dependence of the finite-time dimensionality f PRT on measurement time T and its comparison to data. The large-network limit T ≪N is shown in dashed lines, where the dimensionality increases linearly, same as for independent neurons. For realistic values of the number of neurons N and autocovariance time τ ˜ C, the dimensionality grows sublinearly with rates dependent on the external input strength I. Compared to data measured in monkeys’ PMd and M1 areas while the monkeys perform an eight-direction center-out delayed reach task, the dimensionality can be as low as the experimental values (binned means and standard deviations from data extracted and rescaled from [15], see...

Figure 3: Similarity between activity under two behavioral contexts. A: Schematically, under each behavioral context, there is an ordered response around which temporal chaos fluctuates. B: The ordered-response similarity CS remains close to the similarity between behavioral contexts ρf, decreases only weakly with external input strength, and quickly plateaus, with curves labeled by ρf = cos(nπ/10). A small deviation visible near the chaos-to-order transition reflects the disappearance of temporal chaos. Figure 4: Statistics of activity over multiple behavioral contexts. A: Schematic. Each behavioral context produces an ordered response; the cloud of ordered responses across contexts is approximated as an ellipsoid. B: PR∞increases monotonically with external input strength I, with slow growth at small I and a decreasing growth rate at large I. The vertical dashed line marks the transition from chaotic to ordered dynamics. C: The dependence of the multi-context dimensionality PRNc on context number Nc mirrors that of f PRT on measurement time T. Figure 5: The dimensionality f PR∞is non-monotonic in external input strength I over orders of magnitude of the gain parameter g. The range of external input strength I changes with g because the transition to ordered dynamics varies with g.
